context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about pro vs. anti-development arguments, and I’m kind of stuck on how to frame it. I think the prompt wants more than just “development good/development bad,” but I’m not sure how to organize the historical context part.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — the prompt is definitely asking for more than a simple debate. Let's start with the historical context piece since you flagged it. When you think about the readings from class, when do you recall anti-development arguments gaining traction? What was happening in the world at that time?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of their work and needs orientation. Rather than giving them a framework, I should ask a probing question that helps them identify what they already know about the historical context of anti-development arguments. This uses guided discovery and the Socratic method. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them do the thinking.

exchanges.student: I’m guessing a big wave came after earlier modernization ideas started looking disappointing, like maybe in the 1970s? I vaguely remember criticism tied to colonial legacies, failed state-led development, and maybe frustration with big top-down projects, but I’m not confident how to connect that clearly.
exchanges.tutor: You're pointing in a productive direction. Now try to push that further: what specifically about modernization projects in the postcolonial period made people start questioning whether 'development' itself was the right goal — rather than just questioning the methods?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a reasonable but tentative historical observation. They've identified some relevant elements (modernization critique, colonial legacies, top-down projects) but haven't connected them into a coherent causal story. I should affirm the direction without validating specifics I can't confirm, and push them to think more precisely about the connection between these elements rather than giving them the narrative myself.

exchanges.student: I think maybe because it wasn’t just that some projects failed technically — the whole idea of “becoming modern” started to look imposed and kind of Western-centered. Like if development means other countries have to follow one model, then the critique becomes deeper than “bad implementation,” but I’m still fuzzy on how to say that without sounding too vague.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong insight — the critique shifted from 'how we develop' to 'what development means and who defined it.' Now connect that to the prompt's question: if someone rejects the whole Western-centered model, does that make them utopian, nihilist, or something else entirely? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a significant conceptual leap — identifying that the anti-development critique is epistemological/cultural, not just technical. This is a key insight worth affirming clearly. Now I should push them to connect this insight to the 'utopian or nihilist' question in the prompt, which will help them move from historical context toward the analytical core of the reflection.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it has to be nihilist. It could be a critique of universal models and power, not a rejection of improving people’s lives. Maybe “utopian” fits only if the argument assumes communities can just exist outside states, markets, or global power, but I’m not sure if that’s too harsh.
exchanges.tutor: That's a nuanced distinction and worth keeping. So here's the tension the prompt is really poking at: if someone rejects the dominant development model but still wants to improve lives — can they actually plan? Or does planning itself require buying into some version of the thing they're critiquing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is doing well — they're distinguishing between rejecting a model versus rejecting improvement, and questioning the utopian label critically. This is solid analytical thinking. I should push them toward the next key question in the prompt: can someone hold anti-development views and still plan? This will test whether they can hold the tension between critique and practice.

exchanges.student: I think they probably still can plan, but the planning would look different — more participatory, more locally defined, maybe less obsessed with GDP or “catching up.” I’m a little unsure though, because planning still involves power and the state, so I can see why some anti-development thinkers would distrust planning itself.
exchanges.tutor: You've identified a real tension there — sit with it rather than resolving it too quickly. Now zoom out: given everything you just said, does the pro vs. anti-development divide actually hold up as a useful frame? The prompt asks if it's 'too Manichean' — what do you think that means, and does your reasoning so far support or challenge it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now grappling with a genuine tension — alternative planning vs. the unavoidable role of power and the state. This is directly relevant to the course's central theme about planning, power, and legitimacy. I should affirm this tension as productive and push them to think about whether the pro/anti division itself holds up — nudging them toward the 'too Manichean' question in the prompt.

exchanges.student: I think “too Manichean” means it sets up a fake binary, like either you support development or you reject progress altogether. My reasoning so far kind of challenges the binary, because someone can reject dominant development models but still care about justice, redistribution, and collective decision-making.
exchanges.tutor: That's a coherent and well-reasoned position. You've now got material covering most of the prompt. One piece still missing: the prompt asks about the rise, fall, and latest rise of anti-development arguments. What do you think brought them back recently — what's changed in the world that makes these critiques feel urgent again?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a coherent central argument that challenges the binary. This is the conceptual core of their reflection. We've now covered historical context, the utopian/nihilist question, whether anti-development thinkers can plan, and the Manichean critique. Given the planned conversation length and the rule about redundancy/moving forward, I should now prompt the student to think about the last remaining element: what explains the rise, fall, and latest rise of anti-development arguments. This will complete their conceptual toolkit before they write.

exchanges.student: Maybe the recent rise has to do with disappointment again, but now with neoliberal globalization instead of just modernization theory. I’m thinking inequality, environmental crisis, displacement, debt, and maybe frustration that decades of “development” still left a lot of people vulnerable — is that the right direction?
exchanges.tutor: Strong direction. Notice what you're doing — you're identifying a recurring pattern: dominant development model disappoints, critique intensifies, questions about the model itself emerge. Can you articulate what that pattern suggests about why anti-development arguments rise and fall over time? That could become a strong organizing thread for your reflection.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong connection between the current wave of anti-development critique and the failures of neoliberal globalization. This mirrors the earlier pattern they identified with modernization theory, which shows good analytical consistency. They're now asking for validation. I should affirm the direction and push them to articulate the parallel structure explicitly — this will strengthen their reflection's argument about the cyclical nature of these critiques.

exchanges.student: I think the pattern is that anti-development arguments get stronger when the promises of a dominant model stop feeling believable. Then they fade when a new model appears that seems more humane or effective, but they come back when that model also reproduces inequality or domination. I think that might be my main argument, but I need help turning it into a clean thesis.
exchanges.tutor: That's genuinely strong thinking — you've built that argument yourself through this whole conversation. But writing the thesis is your job, not mine. Take what you just said and try putting it into your own words as a thesis statement. What would that look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong central argument through their own reasoning. Now they're asking me to help them write or polish their thesis, which I must refuse — the rules explicitly state I should not edit, revise, write, or suggest language for the student. I should affirm the strength of their thinking and redirect them to write it themselves, giving them a push toward drafting.

exchanges.student: Maybe: anti-development arguments are neither simply utopian nor nihilist, but recurring critiques that emerge when dominant development models lose legitimacy because they fail materially and politically. I think I also need to add that the pro/anti divide is too simple, since critics can still support planning if it’s more democratic and historically aware.
exchanges.tutor: You've done solid thinking here — your thesis captures the historical pattern, challenges the binary, and leaves room for a nuanced position on planning. It's time to stop refining in conversation and start drafting. Put this on paper and see how it holds up across 700-800 words. What part of the draft do you want to tackle first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has synthesized their thinking into a solid thesis that covers the key elements of the prompt. They're now at the point where they should start drafting. I should affirm the quality of their thinking, note what's working, and push them to begin writing rather than continuing to refine in conversation. This follows the rule about asking students to integrate discussion into written content after sustained productive exchange.

exchanges.student: I think I want to start with the intro, but I’m worried I’ll make it too broad. Could you help me figure out what 2–3 points the intro absolutely needs so I can keep it focused?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you a checklist — but think about it this way: what does a reader need to know upfront in order for your thesis to make sense? What context or framing would make someone say 'ok, I see where this is going'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to scaffold their introduction by listing what it needs. This edges toward doing the work for them, which I should refuse. Instead, I should redirect them to think about what an introduction needs to accomplish given their thesis, using a Socratic question. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.
